title: Security, privacy, and confidentiality of census data | Stats NZ
content:
## Security, privacy, and confidentiality of census data
16 September 2019, 12:00pm
This page explains how we keep the personal information we collect in the census confidential and secure. We must guarantee the security and confidentiality of the information we collect so that people continue to trust and take part in the census.

## Security and confidentiality of census forms
Whether you complete the census online or on paper, we have safeguards and procedures to ensure the security and confidentiality of your information.
We built new systems to run the census online. We checked and tested these systems and processes to ensure they are robust, to meet our commitment to store information securely. When you submit your form, it is secure within our system. The information you enter is encrypted and can only be unlocked when it is within the Stats NZ system. Census data is stored in data centres in the Government private cloud. We do use some public cloud systems, but they are only used for managing the way we run the census.
Our field staff are trained to maintain the security and confidentiality of respondent information at all times. We issue all paper forms with a mailback envelope. When field staff collect completed paper forms, they seal these in an envelope and immediately mail them back to the central storage warehouse. This warehouse is secure and under constant video surveillance. While census forms are processed, we restrict access to the processing system to processing staff and the necessary IT support.
After scanning, we store the information from a paper form as an image and destroy the actual paper forms. We restrict access to archived census forms to a limited number of data archive staff for administrative and technical support purposes.
The Public Records Act 2005 requires us to keep a complete set of census forms. After 100 years, custody of the forms will pass to Archives New Zealand. However, the Government Statistician will still control access to these forms and may allow them to be available for research that meets the confidentiality requirements of the Statistics Act 1975.
For more information about privacy, see:
- Privacy, security, and confidentiality of information supplied to Stats NZ
- 2018 Census independent privacy impact assessment
- How Stats NZ looks after your information .

## Trust and confidentiality
Each census form starts with a confidentiality message from the Government Statistician. All answers you give in the census are confidential to Stats NZ and no other government department or agency – including the New Zealand Police, Immigration New Zealand, and Inland Revenue – has the power to obtain information that could identify you. All Stats NZ employees must sign a declaration of secrecy, which remains in effect for life.
Privacy, security, and confidentiality of information supplied to Stats NZ provides more information about how we safeguard confidentiality, and the special confidentiality requirements for the census.

### Census confidentiality rules
Confidentiality is about how we release findings from data to protect your information from unauthorised people.
Stats NZ confidentialises data for the following reasons:
- to protect individuals from harm
- to maintain the trust and confidence of respondents.
- under our legal obligation within the Statistics Act 1975 and the United Nations Fundamental Principles of Official Statistics.
Each census we review the confidentiality rules that we apply to the release of census data. Census data is high-risk data because it covers the entire country, we publish down to small areas, and there are many interrelated and overlapping tables that can be produced.
We developed seven confidentiality rules with four values in mind: data utility, safety in managing the risks of disclosure, simplicity and practicality in our methods, and consistency across collections and modes of output.
The seven basic confidentiality rules have four relating to counts and three to proportions and measures.
Additional rules cover geospatial confidentiality and configurable tables.
